body:
## Review Summary

The goal of correlating block number with latency is useful for Grafana dashboards, but the custom `call()` override drops the `rpc_request_duration_seconds` histogram that the default implementation records via `record_async_duration` — this is a regression for V4 monitoring (see inline at line 200). Also, this PR only instruments V4, but `main` already has V5 (`NewPayloadV5Request`) for Amsterdam — once Amsterdam activates, V4 stops being called and these metrics go stale.

Consider adding the block-number + latency recording *inside* the existing `handle()` (or as a wrapper around `handle_new_payload_v3`) rather than overriding `call()`, which avoids duplicating the default metrics logic and makes it easy to apply to V3/V5 too.

comments.body:
Bug: by overriding `call()`, the default `record_async_duration(namespace, method, ...)` from `RpcHandler::call` is no longer invoked. This means `engine_newPayloadV4` will stop appearing in the `rpc_request_duration_seconds` histogram — a regression for any existing dashboards or alerts using that metric.

Suggestion: instead of overriding `call()`, add the block-number + latency recording inside `handle()` (which the default `call()` already wraps with duration + outcome tracking). That way you get the new metrics *and* keep the existing ones.

nit: "Both metrics are updated atomically" is misleading — two separate `.set()` calls on different Prometheus gauges are not atomic. A Prometheus scrape that lands between the two `.set()` calls will observe a mismatched (block_number, latency) pair. With a 15s scrape interval this is unlikely to matter in practice, but the comment overpromises. Suggest: "Both metrics are updated together" or just remove the atomicity claim.
